title: Complex Diabetes Management in Advanced (G5 Non-Dialysis) CKD
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: linagliptin across all CKD stages, the SGLT2i 'no evidence' fallacy, GLP-1 RA as the strategic add-on, and safe insulin de-escalation at eGFR 14
authors: Resident training module — illustrative teaching case

patient.chiefComplaint: Optimisation of a complex basal-bolus insulin regimen in advanced diabetic kidney disease.
pmh: Type 2 diabetes on basal-bolus insulin (glargine 18u; aspart 6-6-6u)
medications: Insulin aspart 6 units three times daily with meals

caseDescription: A 65-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, CAD, hypertension and obesity (BMI 32) has advanced diabetic kidney disease — eGFR 14 mL/min/1.73m², CKD G5, not yet on dialysis. He is on a complex basal-bolus insulin regimen (glargine 18u; aspart 6-6-6u) with attendant hypoglycaemia and adherence burden. The teaching question is how to rationalise his non-insulin therapy at eGFR 14: whether linagliptin has a role, whether the resident's claim that SGLT2 inhibitors should be avoided because they are 'not evidence-based' is correct, and how to deploy a GLP-1 receptor agonist to gain cardio-renal benefit, drive weight loss, and safely de-escalate bolus insulin. The fundus is currently normal, raising the early-worsening-retinopathy caveat of rapid glycaemic correction.
keyFindings: Complex basal-bolus insulin with hypoglycaemia and injection burden
CAD + obesity (BMI 32) — strong indications for cardio-renal, weight-lowering therapy
Normal fundus — at risk of early-worsening retinopathy if HbA1c falls rapidly
finalDiagnosis: Type 2 diabetes with advanced (G5 non-dialysis) diabetic kidney disease and established CAD. Plan: continue/optimise basal insulin, add linagliptin 5 mg OD (no dose adjustment at any eGFR), add dulaglutide (REWIND included eGFR ≥15; cardio-renal benefit + weight loss) and taper/eliminate bolus insulin, and withhold SGLT2i at eGFR 14 on an explicit risk-benefit basis (not because 'no evidence exists').
learningObjectives: Explain why linagliptin is the only DPP-4 inhibitor usable across all CKD stages without dose adjustment.
Correct the 'SGLT2i have no evidence in CKD' fallacy and frame the eGFR-14 decision as insufficient evidence with unfavourable risk-benefit.
Select and dose a GLP-1 receptor agonist in advanced CKD (dulaglutide/liraglutide vs semaglutide vs exenatide).
Safely de-escalate bolus insulin when adding a GLP-1 RA, and anticipate early-worsening retinopathy with rapid glycaemic correction.

items.title: Why linagliptin is the only DPP-4i usable at any CKD stage
items.content: Almost all DPP-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin, vildagliptin, saxagliptin, alogliptin) are predominantly renally excreted and require stepwise dose reduction as eGFR falls — a frequent prescribing error in advanced CKD. Linagliptin is the exception: it is eliminated mainly by the hepatobiliary route, so no dose adjustment is needed at any eGFR, including dialysis. At this patient's eGFR of 14 that makes it a clean, low-risk add-on: glucose-dependent action (low intrinsic hypoglycaemia risk), weight-neutral, once daily, with a modest HbA1c reduction of ~0.5-0.8%. CARMELINA confirmed cardiovascular and kidney safety in a high-risk CKD population, and CAROLINA showed CV non-inferiority versus glimepiride with less hypoglycaemia. The practical payoff here is that adding linagliptin may permit a reduction in bolus insulin doses.
keyPoints: Linagliptin = hepatobiliary excretion → no renal dose adjustment, safe even on dialysis.
All other DPP-4i are renally cleared and need dose reduction in CKD.
Low hypoglycaemia risk, weight-neutral, ~0.5-0.8% HbA1c fall (CARMELINA / CAROLINA).

items.title: Why 'no evidence' is the wrong reason to withhold SGLT2i at eGFR 14
items.content: The resident's instinct to withhold an SGLT2 inhibitor here is defensible, but the stated reason — 'not evidence-based' — is wrong and worth correcting precisely. Two facts: (1) the glycaemic effect of SGLT2i is genuinely lost below an eGFR of ~45 because the mechanism depends on filtered glucose, so at eGFR 14 they do not lower glucose — the resident is correct on that point. (2) The cardio-renal benefit, however, is preserved at low eGFR and is squarely evidence-based in the 20-45 range: CREDENCE enrolled down to eGFR 30, DAPA-CKD to 25, and EMPA-KIDNEY to 20, each showing large reductions in kidney-disease progression. At eGFR 14 the data are extrapolated rather than absent, and the real concern is the risk side — volume depletion (on top of antihypertensives), genitourinary infection and euglycaemic DKA, with limited residual benefit if dialysis is near. The correct framing is therefore 'insufficient evidence at eGFR 14 with an unfavourable risk-benefit profile', not 'no evidence'.
keyPoints: Glycaemic benefit is truly lost <eGFR ~45 (mechanism needs filtered glucose) — resident correct here.
Cardio-renal benefit is evidence-based at eGFR 20-45 (CREDENCE 30, DAPA-CKD 25, EMPA-KIDNEY 20).
At eGFR 14: extrapolated, not absent; withhold for risk-benefit (volume depletion, GU infection, DKA), and document the reasoning.

items.title: How to choose and dose a GLP-1 RA in advanced CKD
items.content: A GLP-1 receptor agonist is the strategic add-on for this patient: it addresses obesity (3-6 kg loss), provides proven CV benefit for his CAD, has renal-protective data, can eliminate the bolus insulin burden, and carries low intrinsic hypoglycaemia risk. Agent choice matters at eGFR 14. Dulaglutide is preferred: REWIND enrolled patients down to eGFR ≥15 and showed MACE reduction plus eGFR preservation, with a tolerable GI profile. Liraglutide (LEADER, min eGFR 15) is an alternative but its nausea → dehydration → AKI risk warrants caution and data at eGFR 14 are limited. Semaglutide is not primarily renally cleared and technically needs no dose adjustment, but the landmark renal trial FLOW (2024, ~24% reduction in the kidney composite) excluded eGFR <50 — an important caveat. Exenatide is renally excreted and contraindicated below eGFR 30 — avoid. Across all GLP-1 RA the dominant practical hazard in advanced CKD is GI intolerance causing volume depletion and pre-renal AKI.
keyPoints: Dulaglutide preferred at eGFR 14-15 (REWIND included eGFR ≥15; MACE + eGFR preservation, better GI tolerance).
Semaglutide: not renally cleared, but FLOW excluded eGFR <50; liraglutide ok with caution; exenatide avoid (<30 contraindicated).
Main risk in advanced CKD = nausea/vomiting → volume depletion → AKI.

items.title: How to taper bolus insulin safely when adding a GLP-1 RA
items.content: Insulin simplification is a legitimate therapeutic goal: complex basal-bolus regimens drive hypoglycaemia, and in advanced CKD insulin clearance falls so requirements often decline. The error to avoid is adding a GLP-1 RA while leaving full bolus doses in place — that produces hypoglycaemia, not because of the GLP-1 RA but because the insulin was not reduced. A staged plan for this patient: Week 0, start dulaglutide 0.75 mg weekly and pre-emptively cut each bolus by ~one-third (6-6-6 → 4-4-4); Week 2, review glucose logs and, if post-prandials are controlled, reduce further (2-2-2); Week 4, consider stopping bolus entirely; Week 6, reassess the basal dose, which often also needs lowering; Week 8, full review of HbA1c, weight and tolerability. Continue basal insulin throughout (optimise first), and increase SMBG frequency during the transition.
keyPoints: Reduce bolus pre-emptively when starting a GLP-1 RA — do not wait for hypoglycaemia.
Staged taper: 6-6-6 → 4-4-4 → 2-2-2 → stop, reassessing basal at ~week 6 (insulin clearance falls in CKD).
Optimise/continue basal insulin first; intensify SMBG during transition.

items.title: Why a normal fundus does not make rapid glycaemic correction safe
items.content: Counterintuitively, a normal fundus is not full reassurance. Rapid lowering of a chronically high HbA1c can precipitate early worsening of diabetic retinopathy — a transient phenomenon driven by haemodynamic and growth-factor shifts as glycaemia normalises. SUSTAIN-6 recorded an excess of retinopathy complications in the semaglutide arm, attributed largely to the magnitude and speed of HbA1c reduction in patients with pre-existing retinopathy. The practical implication when a GLP-1 RA plus linagliptin may drop HbA1c substantially: correct gradually rather than abruptly, and arrange ophthalmology review/baseline fundus documentation if a large fall (e.g. >2% absolute, or starting HbA1c >8.5%) is anticipated. The pitfall is assuming 'fundus normal = no retinal risk' and pushing glucose down quickly.
keyPoints: Rapid HbA1c fall can cause early-worsening retinopathy even when the baseline fundus is normal.
SUSTAIN-6 showed increased retinopathy events linked to rapid, large glycaemic improvement.
Correct gradually; loop in ophthalmology if a large HbA1c drop is expected.

items.title: How to apply the CKD-diabetes drug-selection framework by eGFR band
items.content: A simple eGFR-banded mental model prevents most prescribing errors. eGFR ≥45: all agents available. eGFR 30-45: SGLT2i give renal (not glycaemic) benefit; GLP-1 RA retain full benefit; most DPP-4i need dose reduction. eGFR 15-30: SGLT2i may still be considered for cardio-renal benefit; among GLP-1 RA favour dulaglutide or liraglutide; among DPP-4i only linagliptin needs no adjustment. eGFR <15 (this patient): withhold SGLT2i for insufficient data/unfavourable risk-benefit, use dulaglutide with attention to GI tolerance, use only linagliptin among DPP-4i, and expect insulin requirements to fall (reduce doses, watch hypoglycaemia). Metformin is contraindicated at this eGFR. Sulfonylureas (especially long-acting) carry high hypoglycaemia risk in CKD and are best avoided.
keyPoints: eGFR 15-30: SGLT2i for cardio-renal; GLP-1 = dulaglutide/liraglutide; DPP-4i = linagliptin only.
eGFR <15: withhold SGLT2i, dulaglutide (caution GI), linagliptin only, reduce insulin.
Avoid metformin (<30 contraindicated) and long-acting sulfonylureas in advanced CKD.

items.title: The final integrated management plan for this patient
items.content: Putting it together for a 65-year-old man, eGFR 14, BMI 32, CAD and HTN on basal-bolus insulin: (1) Continue and optimise basal insulin glargine 18u — do not stop it. (2) Add linagliptin 5 mg once daily — safe at eGFR 14 with no dose adjustment. (3) Add dulaglutide 0.75 mg weekly (uptitrate to 1.5 mg as tolerated) for combined CV, renal, weight and glycaemic benefit. (4) Reduce then eliminate bolus aspart over 4-8 weeks as above. (5) Withhold SGLT2i at eGFR 14, documenting the risk-benefit reasoning and a plan to reassess if renal function stabilises. (6) Correct glycaemia gradually and arrange ophthalmology review given the normal fundus. Throughout, monitor weight, HbA1c, renal function, hydration status and for GI intolerance.
keyPoints: Continue basal insulin; add linagliptin 5 mg OD; add dulaglutide; taper/stop bolus over 4-8 weeks.
Withhold SGLT2i (document reasoning, reassess); correct glycaemia gradually + ophthalmology review.
Monitor weight, HbA1c, eGFR/creatinine, hydration and GI tolerance.

items.question: Why can linagliptin be used at eGFR 14 without dose adjustment when sitagliptin cannot?
items.answer: Because linagliptin is eliminated predominantly by the hepatobiliary (biliary/faecal) route, its plasma levels are essentially unaffected by renal function, so no dose reduction is required at any eGFR — including dialysis. Sitagliptin and the other DPP-4 inhibitors are predominantly renally excreted, so they accumulate as eGFR falls and require stepwise dose reduction to avoid excess exposure.

items.question: How should bolus insulin be adjusted at the moment a GLP-1 RA is started in this patient?
items.answer: Reduce it pre-emptively rather than waiting for hypoglycaemia. Start dulaglutide 0.75 mg weekly and cut each mealtime aspart dose by roughly one-third immediately (6-6-6 → 4-4-4). Review glucose logs at 2 weeks and reduce further (→ 2-2-2) if post-prandial values are controlled, aim to stop bolus by ~4 weeks, and reassess the basal dose at ~6 weeks since insulin clearance is reduced in advanced CKD. Increase SMBG frequency during the transition.

items.question: A resident says 'SGLT2 inhibitors have no evidence in CKD — avoid.' Critique this statement in three points.
items.answer: 1) Evidence clearly EXISTS for eGFR 20-45 — CREDENCE (down to 30), DAPA-CKD (down to 25) and EMPA-KIDNEY (down to 20) all showed cardio-renal benefit. 2) At eGFR 14 the evidence is limited/extrapolated, not absent. 3) The correct reason to withhold here is 'insufficient evidence at eGFR 14 with an unfavourable risk-benefit profile' (loss of glycaemic effect, volume-depletion/GU-infection/DKA risk, limited benefit if dialysis is near) — not 'no evidence'.

items.question: Why is dulaglutide a reasonable GLP-1 RA choice at eGFR 14-15 whereas exenatide is not?
items.answer: Dulaglutide was studied down to eGFR ≥15 in REWIND (MACE reduction and eGFR preservation) and is not dependent on renal clearance, with a relatively tolerable GI profile. Exenatide is renally excreted and accumulates as eGFR falls; it is contraindicated below eGFR 30 and is therefore inappropriate at eGFR 14.

items.stem: A 65-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and eGFR 14 mL/min/1.73m² needs an oral agent added. Why is linagliptin preferred over sitagliptin in this setting?
options: It is more potent at lowering HbA1c
It requires no renal dose adjustment because it is eliminated by the hepatobiliary route
It produces clinically meaningful weight loss
It has proven superiority for cardiovascular outcomes over sitagliptin
items.explanation: Linagliptin is excreted predominantly via the hepatobiliary route, so no dose adjustment is needed at any eGFR (including dialysis). Other DPP-4 inhibitors such as sitagliptin are renally cleared and require dose reduction. Linagliptin is weight-neutral and offers only a modest HbA1c reduction, and has not shown CV superiority over other gliptins.

items.stem: What was the minimum eGFR for enrolment in the REWIND trial of dulaglutide — the figure that supports its use in this patient?
items.explanation: REWIND enrolled patients down to an eGFR of ≥15 mL/min/1.73m² and showed MACE reduction with eGFR preservation, which is why dulaglutide is a reasonable choice in advanced CKD near this patient's eGFR of 14.

items.stem: The FLOW trial (2024) of semaglutide demonstrated which of the following?
options: A ~24% reduction in a kidney (renal composite) outcome with semaglutide
Superiority of linagliptin over sitagliptin for renal endpoints
Elimination of hypoglycaemia risk when added to basal-bolus insulin
items.explanation: FLOW was the first dedicated renal-outcomes trial of a GLP-1 RA and showed roughly a 24% relative reduction in the renal composite with semaglutide. Note the caveat that FLOW excluded patients with eGFR <50, so it does not directly support use at eGFR 14.

items.stem: For this patient (eGFR 14, CAD, BMI 32, on basal-bolus insulin), which single add-on offers the MOST comprehensive benefit across cardiovascular risk, weight, renal protection and insulin simplification?
items.explanation: Dulaglutide simultaneously addresses CV risk (REWIND MACE reduction), weight (loss in an obese patient), renal protection and allows bolus-insulin de-escalation. Empagliflozin loses glycaemic effect and has unfavourable risk-benefit at eGFR 14; sitagliptin needs dose adjustment and adds little beyond glycaemia; metformin is contraindicated at this eGFR.

items.statement: SGLT2 inhibitors provide meaningful glucose-lowering at an eGFR of 14 mL/min/1.73m².
items.explanation: False. The glucose-lowering action of SGLT2 inhibitors depends on filtered glucose and is largely lost below an eGFR of ~45, so at eGFR 14 they do not meaningfully lower glucose. Their cardio-renal benefit is a separate, eGFR-independent effect (evidence-based down to ~20) but the glycaemic effect is gone.

items.assertion: An SGLT2 inhibitor should be withheld in this patient at eGFR 14.
items.reason: There is no evidence that SGLT2 inhibitors provide any benefit in chronic kidney disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is true (withholding at eGFR 14 is reasonable on a risk-benefit basis), but the reason is false: SGLT2 inhibitors have robust cardio-renal evidence in CKD for eGFR 20-45 (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD, EMPA-KIDNEY). The decision to withhold rests on insufficient evidence and unfavourable risk-benefit specifically at eGFR 14, not on an absence of evidence in CKD. Hence A is true, R is false (option 2).
